family_audit: The exact PTX restore succeeded, so the live surface is back on the clean 24.16-24.18 ms anchor and does not need another recovery-only direction in this round.

Close the exact raw launch-bounds PTX probe and the exact 128x128x32 follow-on as measured negatives, but keep their lessons: lower register budget helps, barrier blowups and 43 KB shared-memory footprints do not.

Promote the non-PTX 128x128 sibling as the next aggressive but bounded occupancy target because it is the closest correctness-proven alternate surface and starts slightly leaner at 196 registers instead of 200.
Keep the microkernel barrier-surgery family alive as the next 128x128 PTX-local aggressive branch after the sibling occupancy probe.
Keep the 256x128 low-register transplant alive as the longer-horizon high-ceiling family.

directions.name: Force 3-CTA Residency On The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The current PTX anchor is fast but structurally pinned at 200 registers, occupancy_limit_registers=2, and about 16.62% active warps. The round-29 PTX launch-bounds probe showed that forcing 3-CTA residency can move the machine state materially, but it also exposed a PTX-specific barrier pathology. The non-PTX 128x128 sibling is the cleanest next branch to carry that occupancy experiment because it is already a correctness-proven alternate surface at 24.183295 ms and starts slightly leaner at 196 registers with nearly the same plateau signature. Restoring the exact sibling surface and then forcing its hot-band kernel toward 3 resident CTAs is therefore the best bounded aggressive test: it asks whether the occupancy win survives better on the non-PTX control/export path than it did on the PTX microkernel path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and latency hiding on the non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. Correctness risk is low because the base sibling surface is already known-good, but performance risk remains because forcing a 3-CTA budget can still trade registers for spills or expose new synchronization costs.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime with a >0.15 ms improvement threshold over the current 24.168880 ms run
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest way to keep pushing the occupancy thesis without repeating the exact PTX-local failure mode from round 29.
The sibling path is close enough to the current anchor that the implementation can stay bounded to a restore plus launch-budget change.
The user guidance favors this kind of structural probe over another plateau replay.
directions.stop_condition: Stop this family if launch__registers_per_thread does not fall materially below the sibling baseline or if occupancy rises but barrier cost explodes into another round-29 style regression.

directions.name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
directions.hypothesis: Round 29 and round 30 fenced off the two obvious extremes on the PTX 128x128 occupancy branch. The launch-bounds probe proved that 168 registers and about 24.8% active warps are reachable, but barrier stall doubled. The two-K-stage follow-on tried to amortize that barrier, but the 43,008 B shared-memory footprint erased the occupancy gain. The next PTX-local aggressive family is therefore a narrower surgery: start from the restored PTX anchor, keep the smaller 22,016 B shared-memory footprint, and trim the wait/sync cadence or export handoff so barrier cost falls without paying the x32 shared-memory tax.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel while preserving the small shared-memory footprint.
directions.risk: High. This is a microkernel-level control-flow surgery path rather than a simple restore-plus-launch-bounds probe.
ranking_notes: Keep this family above the 256x128 transplant because it now has two rounds of direct causal evidence behind it.
Do not auto-select it first because the non-PTX sibling occupancy probe is the cleaner next bounded experiment.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit drifts back into x32-like shared-memory inflation or fails to reduce barrier stall materially from the round-29 10.97% level.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 family still represents the largest visible occupancy ceiling in repo history, because its half-panel repairs reached about 92-93 registers and about 32.9% active warps even though the writer split made those snapshots unusable. That remains worth keeping in the live queue, but it stays third because the code motion is broader and the correctness history is worse than either 128x128 follow-up family.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and synchronization strategy on the 256x128 hot-band path, plus correctness-sensitive writer ownership.
directions.risk: High. This remains the broadest and least stable of the active aggressive families.
ranking_notes: Keep this family in queue because it is still the only branch that ever showed low-30% active warps on this shape.
Rank it below both 128x128 follow-ups because the next round should still prefer bounded aggressive probes over another broad correctness-risky rewrite.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on correctness failure or on another 28-30 ms class regression without a decisive occupancy breakthrough.

notes: Round 32 starts from a recovered clean base, so the ranking can go back to fully aggressive directions instead of spending a slot on recovery. The key evidence from the last three rounds is now coherent: the accepted PTX anchor remains around 24.17 ms at 200 registers and about 16.6% active warps; the PTX launch-bounds probe proved that lower-register 3-CTA residency can move the machine state but suffered a barrier blowup; the x32 follow-on proved that barrier amortization cannot come from doubling shared memory to 43 KB. The next bounded aggressive probe should therefore test the same occupancy thesis on the correctness-proven non-PTX 128x128 sibling before reopening broader or more manual branches.
